data.title: AMD Stock: Why I'm Buying Before Earnings
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) stock has had an eventful few months as the April tariff war led to a significant decline, followed by a major run-up to 52-week highs on optimism over the company's AI prospects, and finally to a respite in the $150 range as investors prepare for what's next. I have been bullish throughout this rollercoaster, and continue to see AMD as a top stock over the next decade.

In my last piece on AMD, I cited the strong performance of the company's GPUs, server CPUs, and consumer CPUs as the main basis for my bull thesis, leading to a Strong Buy recommendation. Shares subsequently rose more than 50% over the next 2 months, before settling back to a 30%+ gain as some investors took profits after the rapid price increase. That article can be read here.

With shares now loitering below 52-week highs, shareholders are likely wondering -- what comes next? Before we can try to answer that, let's quickly recap AMD's most recent quarterly results, it's Q2 report released back in early August:

As we can see, revenue continues to grow at a rapid pace (+32% YoY/+3% QoQ) as all of AMD's significant product lines have found success in both the enterprise and consumer markets. The company's Instinct GPUs have gained a foothold in the data center as companies explore alternatives to Nvidia's (NVDA) pricey Hopper and Blackwell GPUs, its EPYC server CPUs are dominating as the chips have lapped Intel's lackluster offerings, and its Zen 5 consumer CPUs, especially those with 3D V-Cache, have proven to be quite popular.

While the rest of the income statement looks quite dire, these numbers include an $800 million inventory charge related to U.S. export control restrictions on shipments to China of AMD's MI308X GPUs, which are a less powerful version of the MI300X. Without this charge, non-GAAP gross margin was 54% (+100 bps YoY) and similarly, EPS would have been $0.91 or +32% YoY, indicating that operating leverage has remained intact.

While the U.S. government eventually relented on this restriction, which should have cleared this inventory charge and allowed AMD to resume selling MI308X GPUs in China, the Chinese government on Wednesday announced that it will ban companies from purchasing Nvidia GPUs in order to encourage adoption of domestic AI accelerators, which could mean that these one-time charges are back on the table. Oddly enough, the Chinese government has not yet issued a similar ban on AMD's MI308X, but it seems likely this will occur in the coming weeks. Regardless, even without revenue from China, the metrics point to a healthy income statement when considering organic fundamentals.

On segment-specific performance, AMD demonstrated growth and further inroads in all areas: Data Center sales were up 14% YoY even factoring in lost revenue from the MI308X, Client revenue was a record $2.5 billion and rose a whopping 67% YoY, and Gaming revenue, which includes discrete consumer GPUs and semi-custom console processors, was up 73% YoY. We'll of course get to the data center in a bit, but I'd like to call attention to just how successful AMD's "supporting cast" Client & Gaming segment has been during this resurgence:

Operating margins have expanded significantly, from 8% to 21% YoY and quarterly operating income is closing in on $1 billion, which is impressive for a part of the company most people consider an afterthought. While Gaming revenue is lower-margin, lumpy, and fluctuates with the console cycle, the Client portion of the business has proven to be a resounding success and I think we'll see operating margin for the overall segment increase as operating leverage increases and as the product mix improves (i.e. more Client, less Gaming revenue).

And the last bit of interesting numbers we got for Q2 was AMD's outlook for Q3 2025:

The company expects revenue of $8.7 billion at the midpoint (+27.5% YoY/+13.1% QoQ), gross margin to remain flat (excluding inventory charge), and EPS of around $1.15 (+25% YoY/+26% QoQ) as the upward catalysts that have lifted AMD's operating results in recent quarters are projected to continue.

Now let's discuss what's on everyone's mind -- the data center. Nvidia is a $4 trillion stock because of this market and this is where AMD has been fighting tooth and nail in an attempt to gain a more substantial foothold in order to secure some of the spoils for itself. The company's Instinct family of GPUs, which are primarily geared towards accelerating AI workloads, is the spearhead of that attempt.

The current generation is the MI300 line of GPUs, which has put the company on the map in the inferencing space and has attracted the interest of hyperscalers like Microsoft (MSFT), Oracle (ORCL), Meta (META) and more, even as it has lagged Nvidia's Hopper and Blackwell processors. However, I think AMD is rapidly closing the gap with Nvidia as it has pursued aggressive timelines and ambitious performance improvements for its latest releases, namely the newly released MI355X. AMD promised that the jump from MI325X to MI355X would be much more significant than the relatively small jump from the MI300X to the MI325X.

Here's a quote from a previous article of mine comparing this chip to previous iterations:

AMD pulled the release date up from 2H2025 to mid-year 2025 to better compete with Nvidia's Blackwell family of GPUs, and built its feature set around this niche. The processor will support the company's new CDNA 4 architecture, which adds support for FP6 and FP4 precision, sports a ~1.8x compute performance increase over the MI325X, operates with 8 TB/sec bandwidth (about the same as a Blackwell B200), and has a whopping 288 GB of HBM3E memory relative to 192 GB for a B200.

The ramp of this new GPU, which AMD claims is garnering significant interest from hyperscalers for large deployments, is the main driver for the company's strong Q3 guide and will determine revenue growth and margin potential for 2H2025. I am very bullish on its prospects as AMD has stepped up its game to deliver an inferencing workhorse that challenges Nvidia in total cost of ownership even if performance remains a bit below the latter's latest generations. This is the same strategy AMD used against Intel to great effect.

In that vein, a few days after AMD's Q2 report, Mercury Research released its quarterly market and revenue share update for the CPU space:

We can see that desktop gains continued by leaps and bounds as unit share (+9.2% YoY/4.2% QoQ) and, more importantly, revenue share (+20.5% YoY (!)/+4.9% QoQ) exploded higher on Zen 5's success and Intel's inability to field affordable, performant offerings. The server market shows more of the same as, even though share gains are slowing, AMD has taken a commanding position in unit share (+3.2% YoY/+0.1% QoQ) and revenue share (+7.2% YoY/1.5% QoQ), with the latter increases further demonstrating that the company's products are now becoming more entrenched as the premium processors in the space.

Lastly, I'd like to address the big news this week that Intel and Nvidia are teaming up to create processors for the consumer and enterprise markets using Intel CPUs and Nvidia GPUs on SoCs connected by NVLink. I discussed the implications of this deal for Intel and Nvidia in an article on Thursday, which can be read here, but how does AMD play into all of this?

An initial gut reaction might be to say that AMD's two biggest rivals teaming up to create processors would be a very bad thing, and on paper that could be the case. However, while there is potential disruption to AMD's momentum here, this looks more like political posturing from Nvidia, in my opinion, than an attempt to make waves in the market as becoming a joint partner with the US government in Intel, the cornerstone of the administration's push for domestic chip production, is a smart way for Nvidia to mitigate potential political pressure down the road.

However, Nvidia and Intel have said that these chips will be manufactured at TSMC (TSM), not Intel Foundry, which still leaves the latter a question mark despite the US government's strong insistence that domestic chip production be protected. This leads me to believe that these processors might not be for volume production or that they might be a staging ground for Intel to eventually transition them to its 18A or 14A process should they reach sufficient yields, which is not a guaranteed outcome considering Intel's recent manufacturing struggles. And even if this all works out as planned, these processors are years away from entering the market and Nvidia says it plans to maintain its ARM roadmap as well, so the effects on AMD will not be felt for a while, if they're felt at all.

Investor Takeaway

AMD shares have taken a breather after a significant run-up as data center performance, specifically sales of the MI355X, comes into focus in 2H2025. Beyond 2025, the company's MI400 series promises even larger jumps in inferencing workloads as the AI arms race ramps up and the market expands. Despite the recent increase in valuation, I think AMD presents a solid risk-reward profile for patient investors.

In the short term, I expect we will see some volatility going into the end of 2025 as the AI wave has reached a crescendo and expectations for AMD's GPU prospects are running high.

That said, I like the company's position heading into this gauntlet and I will be adding to my holdings Friday in anticipation of a bump into Q3 earnings. Long term, I continue to see AMD as a winner in the processor space on the back of increasing AI market share, GPU and CPU expertise, and an attractive valuation relative to other names in the market; therefore I am reiterating a Strong Buy.

Thanks for reading!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Inside The PRO Quant Portfolio
data.description:
Click here to learn more about Seeking Alpha’s bundle promo!

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Emma Johnston: Hello, and welcome to the Leveraging PRO Quant Portfolio Webinar. My name is Emma Johnston. I'm a Quantitative Strategist here at Seeking Alpha. And I am here today with Steven Cress, our VP of Quantitative Strategy here at Seeking Alpha. And we're going to go through all things PQP or PRO Quant Portfolio today, that's our latest investment product.

Really quickly, let me get this disclaimer out of the way. The PRO Quant Portfolio is not an investment product run with real money. It is not a brokerage account and neither enables nor reflects actual trading activity. No recommendation or advice is given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. You are solely responsible for determining whether an investment security, strategy, product, or service is appropriate or suitable for you based on your investment objectives and personal and financial situation and for evaluating its benefits and risks.

Seeking Alpha is not a fiduciary by virtue of any person's use of or access to the site. Any views or opinions expressed in the webinar do not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Any content and tools on the platform are offered for information purposes only. Seeking Alpha does not take account of your objectives or your financial situation and does not offer any personalized investment advice. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, U.S. investment adviser or investment bank.

All right.

Steven Cress: Emma, can you say that one more time? I think I missed it.

EJ: Someone on the chat popped in while I was reading and I was like, oh no, I've lost where I am, which reminds me, everyone, the chat is open. We are leaving some time at the end of the discussion today to go over questions. So please bring those forward. I will be keeping an eye on that throughout the presentation.

Really quickly about myself. I'm a Quantitative Strategist here at Seeking Alpha. I contribute to the stewardship of the Alpha Picks and PQP portfolios. I have about eight years of experience in systematic equity strategies, and I've most previously worked at an – as an investment strategist for an asset manager. Steve, can you tell the folks about yourself?

SC: I can indeed, and thank you. So I am at Seeking Alpha as the Head of Quantitative Strategies. Seeking Alpha purchased my company about six years ago, and I created a technology, which basically was really sort of a preliminary form of AI and quantitative systems. And we developed a system where you could put the name of any company in and get a fresh 25 page research report. Once I integrated the technology into Seeking Alpha, you will see it in its current format now. The bulk of my career was spent in quantitative finance. I spent about 14 years at Morgan Stanley, where I ran a prop trading desk in quantitative strategies. I was also the Founder of a hedge fund in quantitative strategies. And in total, well over 30 years of experience in the world of finance between Wall Street and working in the city of London.

EJ: Amazing, quite the tenure there. We're super lucky to have your expertise.

SC: Thank you.

EJ: Just very briefly, today, this presentation is going to cover why Quant, so it's the Quant system that powers the PRO Quant portfolio. Then we're going to introduce the PRO Quant portfolio and some of its portfolio highlights and talk about other PRO capabilities.

So Steve, with that, I'm going to hand it off to you. Can you give us a bit of a Quant overview?

SC: Absolutely. Thanks. So when I think of Quant, I do often tell people it is really similar to what an investment analyst does. If you're a research analyst that worked at Morgan Stanley or Goldman Sachs or Merrill Lynch, they have a way that they conduct research on companies and come up with a directional Buy, Sell, or Hold recommendation.

What we do is very similar on the Quant side. However, we have the capabilities of using computer processing to make the job a lot easier and cover far more companies than any one single analyst can do. And the way we do that is we design our quantitative strategies around math and algorithms to identify opportunities.

Now, not all Quant strategies are the same. We use what I refer to as a GARP strategy, which is growth at a reasonable price plus. Some Quant strategies are strictly Momentum, some are strictly Value, some are strictly Growth. We use a diversified approach. And in that diversified approach, the core investment characteristics would be looking for companies that are collectively strong on Value, Growth, Profitability, Momentum, and upward Analyst EPS Revisions. And that's actually the quantity of analysts that are taking the revisions up for a stock.

So when we take those investment characteristics and we create algorithms around it, it gives us the ability to sort thousands and thousands of stocks on a daily basis. So we can actually rank all the companies relative to each other in a sector and come up with the strongest companies and the weakest companies.

So that's a little bit about how Quant works and our strategy has worked very well. We have a track record. So what you're looking at now is if we were to actually take all the Quant Strong Buys every day and measure their performance, our track record actually goes back to 2010. But I've taken the last five years because a good portion of that is a backtest.

However, over the last five years, it's actually simulated trades. So you could see on the far left-hand side, the Quant performance for our Strong Buys is up 265% versus Wall Street analysts up 52%, and the S&P 500 for the same period up 83%. So this is really not exactly an investable product because we're looking at all the Quant Strong Buys. And on any given day, there could be anywhere from 350 Strong Buys to 400 Strong Buys.

So in fact, you'd have to be a computer model yourself just to execute all those trades. And here is actually the year-to-date performance. So that went back five years. This gives you an idea of our more recent history. Year-to-date, you could see our Quant Strong Buys overall are up 22%, Wall Street Strong Buys are up 14%, and the S&P 500 for the same period up 8.4%.

I think what's interesting about this is Wall Street actually typically lags the S&P 500, but this year, they're doing particularly well. And I think it's because the markets have brought it out. We don't have really the same appreciation that and concentration - we do have the concentration risk in the Mag 7 stocks still, but they're not providing the same performance that they have over recent years. So it's given Wall Street analysts the chance to sort of outperform, but not outperform our Quant system, which is up 22% year-to-date.

So that brings us to the PRO Quant Portfolio. And one of the reasons why the PRO Quant Portfolio exists, along with another product that we have called Alpha Picks, is as I mentioned, you really can't invest in 400 or 350 stocks on a daily basis.

So what we've done, particularly with a PRO Quant Portfolio, is we're taking our top 30 Quant Strong Buys. We've created a portfolio out of it. And I'm going to give you a little bit of details on the PRO Quant Portfolio, which we just launched in the beginning of June, on June 1st.

So one of the reasons why we launched it is we ran a back test on this product. And the back test, which goes from January of 2015 to May of 2025, was up 1,632% versus the S&P for the same period up 162%. So this is a portfolio of 30 stocks that gets rebalanced on a weekly basis. And as you can see, our top 30 Strong Buys have performed extremely well during that back test period.

So we indeed launched the product. The official date, I guess, you could say it was May 30th of 2025. And just between May 30th and September 18th, the PRO Quant Portfolio is up a whopping 41% compared to the S&P 500 on an equal weighted basis up 6.49%. And on a market cap weighted basis, the S&P is up 11.65%. And the reason why we showed on an equal weighted basis is because the PRO Quant Portfolio is equal weighted. So it's sort of an apples-to-apples comparison to the benchmark. And as you can see, the performance has been absolutely fantastic since we launched the product.

A little bit on the portfolio construction and how it works. So we're choosing our top 35 – our top 30 stocks out of a universe of nearly 5,000 stocks. And as I mentioned, we're looking for companies out of those 5,000 that are collectively strong on those core investment characteristics of Value, Growth, Profitability, Momentum, and EPS Revision.

Again, it's equally weighted, the rebalance happens every Monday at the open of the New York Stock Exchange at 9:30. And when we do it, we actually use the prior Friday's closing price. And the reason why we have to use the prior Friday's closing price is we actually need that price to select the stocks. So the stocks get selected over the weekend based on the closing prices from Friday. And we use the closing prices on Friday to determine what the weights in the portfolio will be Monday at 9:30.

I should add that we are actually going to change this strategy shortly, by popular demand. Many of our subscribers to PRO wanted to use VWAP over the course of Monday, which is something similar to what we do with Alpha Picks. So we will be changing that strategy.

It doesn't really impact the algorithm of the stock selection, but it does change the price that's used. So instead of using a price on the Friday close, we'll use the VWAP on a Monday. And we actually won't display it until Tuesday, along with the weights until Tuesday, because obviously, trading has to close on Monday in order to get a VWAP.

And VWAP is called volume weighted average price. So we're taking the average price over the course of the day. And a lot of individuals felt when we showcase performance, that would just be a fair way to do it. So their performance could be closer to that of the portfolio.

Going off to our next slide. When we buy and sell stocks, so a stock has to be a Strong Buy to come into the portfolio. It could be any stock in the U.S. or any ADR across the world. We look at all market capitalization levels. The – some of the criteria that would prevent it is if there was an SEC delay in reporting, we wouldn't want that type of company in the portfolio. The stock does have to have a certain amount of liquidity to make the portfolio. And you'll see on the final bullet point on the left-hand side, we do add stocks that are under $10. They are permitted. And the reason why I have that in there is for our Alpha Picks portfolio, that is a parameter and we do not allow stocks in under $10.

When do we sell stocks from the portfolio? We sell it the day of if it falls to a Sell or a Strong Sell. We will remove a stock if it drops to a Buy or Hold only after a consecutive number of trading days, and that number of trading days is proprietary to us. So we don't really unveil that. If any M&A thresholds are breached, we would take a stock out of the portfolio. And similar to if we have a stock in the portfolio, if there is an SEC filing delay, we would take it out of the portfolio.

So we often get questions in terms of our small cap recommendations. So some of the companies have market caps under 500 million or under 100 million. And often when we recommend those stocks, there's a lot of investors who are trying to buy it at the same time.

So this would happen with any firm and to much larger extent of a small cap stock was recommended by Morgan Stanley or Goldman Sachs, that would put a lot of pressure on the stock price and go far more than Seeking Alpha recommending it. But what I do is I tell people that they can always sort of put a limit on the stock.

So if you do have a company that's got a market cap under 500 million, you'd probably want to put a limit order in and possibly wait for retracement if you miss it, or you could set limit orders at various levels so you don't have to fill your entire order the same day.

So by example, say, you were going to buy 100 shares of the stock, you could put in for, on that Monday, 25 shares, the following day, 25 shares, so on and so on until you fill the full amount that you want. So this is often an intelligent way to purchase small cap stocks that are being recommended by whether it be Seeking Alpha or a larger investment bank.

There is a big benefit for having small cap stocks. It does add by diversification. We're a big fan of diversification at Seeking Alpha, whether it's Alpha Picks or PQP. I'm a firm believer that diversification does minimize risk and maximizes returns over the long period of time. And those small cap stocks, even though they might be volatile in the beginning, over long-term, they tend to really, really pay off. And it helps you build a basket of high-quality stocks with small, medium, and large cap stocks. So if you like a company's fundamentals, but it's small, just enter into it gradually and build your basket.

So if you're putting fresh capital to work, we often get the question, what is the best way to do it with PQP? People will say, do I have to buy all 30 stocks? It's important to keep in mind that the PRO Quant Portfolio is a portfolio of investment research ideas.

So you certainly do not have to buy all the stocks. If you feel uncomfortable with some of the ADRs, or if you feel uncomfortable with small cap stocks, you do not have to purchase it. It also depends on your risk tolerance, the amount of capital that you have.

So if you start, you could take five to 10 Strong Buy-rated stocks, or you can allocate a small amount of capital across the entire portfolio, or you could just cherry pick names. You can look over that and just pick one or two stocks. So there's any number of strategies that you can use when you engage with the PRO Quant Portfolio.

And Emma, I think from there, I'll sort of hand it over to you so you can refer – you can review some of the – our best performers and in all fairness, some of our worst performers.

EJ: Yeah, excellent. So thank you so much, Steve. We did want to point out some of our top and bottom performers for the PQP portfolio. This is something if you're an Alpha Pick subscriber, we like to highlight this on a biweekly basis.

I guess two things to note. One, this does include stocks that have since been sold. So you'll see Willdan there and the top performer was added to the portfolio on May 30th and was sold August 15th, and in that time returned an incredible 108%. But the two stocks above that CommScope and China Yuchai International Limited are both still open. So they're still Quant-rated Strong Buys and adding returns to the portfolio.

And then in terms of our bottom performers, you can see at the bottom of the page, those have all since been sold, but these are the biggest detractors from the portfolio since its inception. And I think the important thing to highlight here is just the orders of magnitude by which the top performers are outpacing the bottom performers.

So if you're familiar with our Alpha Picks portfolio, not all stocks are winners, but the winners do tend to significantly outperform the losers. And I think that same principle applies here for the PQP portfolio. Again, greater frequency of stock ideas. So in theory, more exposure to potential winners.

I wanted to highlight a couple of our Strong Buys on the next page, Steve. Like I mentioned, CommScope, this was one of the stocks that is a top performer that was on the last slide, but this just kind of gives you a flavor of what informs the selection.

So as Steve mentioned, these stocks are scored collectively across their fundamental indicators, which are Value, Profitability, Growth, and then their timeliness indicators, which are EPS Revisions and Momentum.

So you can see that Factor Grade scorecard to the right of the image there that highlights the Valuation, Growth, Profitability, Momentum, and Revisions for CommScope. And those all roll up into that Quant Strong Buy rating.

Here, I've highlighted some things about the company. It's on the smaller side, $3.61 billion information technology company out of the entire universe of IT stocks, it's rated number two and that's out of 541. That's a nice Premium feature.

For Premium subscribers, you'd be familiar with the fact that you can just kind of drill into these names and get every statistic you'd really want about them, including how they rank from a sector and industry perspective.

So top communications equipment sector, or excuse me, communications equipment stock, one out of 40, they basically build and support technology infrastructure that powers the Internet wireless and business connectivity, a huge servicer of data centers, which has been a boon for this stock.

Just wanted to highlight, you can see from the Factor score card that it, which are – these Factor Grades are scored on a sector relative basis. I think that's important to highlight, but demonstrates really impressive Profitability and Growth.

So in terms of growth, 62% year-over-year EBITDA growth, that's 528% above the IT sector. From a profitability perspective, they've got $200 million in cash from operations. That's 134% above the sector, and it's trading at a nice discount at a 13x price-to-earnings. That's a 49% discount to the sector.

So those are some of the underlying metrics that help inform those Factor Grades. And I just wanted to pull forth those highlights, again, return an incredible 130% to the PQP portfolio since its addition back at inception, starting at the close on May 30th.

So our next slide…

SC: And these three stocks are still in the portfolio, correct? So…

EJ: Yes.

SC: …you're giving away three of our top ideas here.

EJ: Yeah, yeah, yeah. You're – all the folks who are smart enough to join us, get a little peek inside the portfolio today. So another Quant Strong Buy that's still in the portfolio and was also added at inception is SSR Mining. This really evidences the strength of the Quant model, which started picking up on the rally in the materials sector.

So we saw gold and silver stocks just take off. I mean, they've been doing well all year, but particularly accelerated at the start of summer. So SSR Mining is a gold miner with operations across the globe. Again, a bit on the smaller side, but at 4.42 billion, highly ranked materials, highly ranked gold stock, incredible growth, 23% forward EPS, 164% above the sector.

This is something that's great about the underlying metrics. You can look at growth in the near-term and further out. So from a long-term growth perspective, it shows 92% EPS forward long-term growth on a three- to five-year CAGR basis. That's 662% above the sector. So way higher.

It's trading at a 0.2x PEG, price-to-earnings growth. So that's an 89% discount to the materials sector. And in terms of its earnings estimates revisions, it's received 8 FY1 up revisions in the last 90 days versus 0 downwards. So again, since it was added, it has returned nearly 80% to the PQP portfolio. So it's a nice mining option we've had for subscribers. And then lastly…

SC: Emma I'll actually- I want to highlight on the Factor Grade card. As Emma mentioned, it's sector relative. So despite the fact that the stock is up 80% since the end of May, you could see the Valuation Grade on this is a B+ versus six months ago, it was an A-. So the Valuation framework is almost exactly the same as it was six months ago, despite the stock being up so significantly.

And then you could look underneath that Valuation, you see Growth is at an A+, which is the same as it was six months ago. So that means relative to the sector, it is – its Growth is way, way higher. And relative to the sector, the Valuation framework is definitely more attractive.

So relative to the sector and its own historical average compared to six months ago, the stock still looks great. So you shouldn't be afraid of the fact that it's up almost 80% because that Valuation framework is still there.

Emma, I'll hand it back to you so you could show the number three stock.

EJ: No problem. Thanks for pointing that out. And yeah, I just wanted to round out a third option, TTM Technologies here. This is another IT stock and electronic manufacturing services stock. Again, similarly sized to the other two, but it specializes in mission systems, radio frequency, and RF microwave, microelectronic assemblies.

Again, we're - adjacent to the aerospace and defense as well as data center sectors. So you're getting some of that indirect exposure to AI growth, certainly been a boon for this company. 25% forward EPS growth, that's more than 100% – 122% versus the IT sector, 34% forward operating cashflow growth, 131% above the sector. And then in terms of Profitability, $4.34 in cash per share, that's a 100% above the sector.

I just saw a question come through, someone asking if this is summarized anywhere and this exact - the way this information has been tabulated is not exactly summarized on the platform, but you can definitely glean all this information on the Premium platform by drilling into those individual Factor Grades. And you'll see, it's just a wealth of information. We're talking hundreds of data points that inform these Factor Grades, and you can see much more than what I'm actually showing you here.

SC: Emma, I'll take that. I'll go into the platform and we'll give people a peek and see what it's like. So right now, we're going to go to duplicate that, and we're going to put in a ticker symbol. We'll put in SSRM, which Emma just reviewed.

So right now we're on the PRO platform. And what you're seeing here is probably very similar to the Premium platform as well. So in regards to that question specifically, it is really easy. You're within one click of seeing all those underlying metrics.

So I'm going to click on growth for SSRM. And you could see we have an abundance of growth metrics, both year-over-year and the forward estimates as well for revenue, for EBITDA, for EBIT, for earnings per share, and we break it down to a diluted basis, a GAAP basis. We look at leverage free cash flow, free cash flow per share, operating cash flow, operating cash flow forward, ROE growth rate. So there's just a whole host of metrics you can see.

The Factor Grades, the intention there is to give you an instant characterization of how that company compares to the sector. But we're very transparent, so we actually show you the data points as well.

So by example, on the top row, you see the revenue growth rate of 6.2%. To the right of that, you'll see for the sector median, it's 2%. So the revenue growth rate for the company is at 192% premium to the sector. And then we show you what it is compared to its five-year average as well. That's for Growth.

If I were to go into Value, you would see the same thing. So if we look at P/E on a forward basis, it's a B. In absolute terms, it is 13.89x. That would be the P/E versus the sector at a P/E of 16x. So it's at a discount to the sector by 14%. And then if you look at on a GAAP basis, it's actually at a discount to the sector on 34%. Hence why the overall Valuation is a B+ saying that this is a better Valuation compared to the sector.

So I just wanted you to know ton of metrics there. So you can see for P/E, PEG, EV-to-sales, EV-to-EBITDA, EV-to-EBIT, price-to-sales, price-to-book, price-to-cash flow, all those metrics there for you to see exactly how it compares to the sector.

And I will take us back to the presentation, Emma. And I believe you finished up on that slide. Is that right?

EJ: Yeah.

SC: Yeah.

EJ: I think we're done with the Strong Buys.

SC: Okay.

EJ: And I just wanted to mention how to follow trade alerts. I've seen some questions come through in the chat about the process. And Steve mentioned on the top of the conversation that we will be changing - currently, the analysis is published at 9.30 AM on Mondays. The portfolio is rebalanced weekly. And so you will see the Buys and Sells for the week at that time. Until we make the change wherein the portfolio will be, the trades will be published at 12:00 noon on Mondays. And then the performance will be calculated based on the volume weighted average price on Tuesdays. That's all to say, this you know, you will receive alerts on a weekly basis. So that is the quickest way to see what the trades are. Be on the PQP platform at 9:30 AM on Mondays to see which trades have been made. And if you like to short stocks or if you're a hedge fund, we have some great short ideas that come in from our contributors that you can't even get on Wall Street.

I want to take us to the platform. So we will go back to where you see SSR Mining. And if you looked on the left-hand rail, you'll see we have News, we have Market Data, and here are some of the features that I was referring to if we go to Top Analysts and I click on that. This will show you the top 15 analysts. And we actually, this is a somewhat of a leaderboard that we keep, we only show the top 15. And we measure it, they have to have at least five ideas that they bring forward, and we track them over the course of a 12-month period.

So it's at least five ideas or more over a 12-month period. This really gives you breadth in terms of the ones that have good recommendations and their success rate. Instead of just like saying, somebody could just pick one stock, we're trying to avoid that.

So we want to make sure that they have at least five ideas. And you could see this individual, he has a 100% success rate. Average return has been 88% on eight of his recommendations. So that is very good. That's actually a little bit stronger, even than PQP. So if you're a PRO customer, you get that as an added benefit.

And then if you click on Top Analyst Ideas on that left hand menu, this will basically show you as the ideas come in. So we've had quite a few articles that were written on the 17th, on the 16th, on the 15th. So we will show the ideas that are coming in from our top analysts. Again, these features are not available on the Premium platform. And then, of course you have PRO Quant Portfolio.

So as Emma mentioned, you could wait for the email or you could be here Monday morning at 9:30. And as soon as the stocks are released, it will be here at 9:30. And you could just click on it. We're very transparent. We show the articles for every single company that we have written about. And then if you take a look at the portfolio performance, we can actually sort it here.

And you can see we've had some incredible winners that came into the portfolio in the beginning of May, as Emma highlighted, CommScope Holding and CYD, both up over 100%. We have quite a few stocks that are up over 50%, as you can see the portfolio. And I'm going to go on to another tab because it will actually show you, oh, it's on this tab here, you can actually see the sector.

So we have quite a few in financials, quite a few in information technology. We got a handful, good handful in industrials. And as you can see, there are quite a number of materials stocks. Now this does change, but what the model did a great job of doing, and you can see many of these materials stocks are up double digits. New Gold up 43%, SSR up 81%, Kinross Gold up 49%, Gold Fields Limited up 60%. And the model does a great job of really finding companies that are collectively strong on Value and Growth at the same time.

So if you have great growth rates and the values are really attractive, many of those stocks will come to the top of the model. Now the model is, it does work on a bottom-up basis. So we are looking at the fundamentals of companies. It's not top-down, but when you have a particular sector that's doing well, the cream rises to the top and it shows up in our model.

You can also see here the performance overall. So we're on the PRO Quant Portfolio platform right now. And if you click on, say, one month, you could see the performance of the portfolio over one-month period. It's up 12.4% versus the S&P up 1.2%. And it doesn't go back year-to-date, but this shows you from the inception point.

So that's a little bit about the portfolio. Very transparent. You could see the date that it's picked, you could see the price that it was picked at, you could see the weight it has in the portfolio, and you could see the Quant ratings as well and the level of strength for that Quant rating.

So not all Strong Buys are created equally. There is a weight behind it. And this actually shows you what the weight is. And, of course, you could see what the price return is. And not all of them have won. If I click here, obviously, you could see the winners have been fantastic. But there are a few losers too, not many. And they really have not gone down that much compared to the winners in the portfolio.

Emma, I'm going to take us back to the slideshow. And we often get a lot of questions, what is the difference between the PRO Quant Portfolio and Alpha Picks? Alpha Picks was developed about three years ago. And that came at a time where again, not really investing in all the Quant Strong Buys, it wasn't an option. So we came through with a product that just highlighted our top two Quant recommendations every month.

So on the trading date closest to the first of the month and the 15th of the month, we send out an email and Alpha Picks has its own platform. So that is different where the PRO Quant Portfolio, we launched with 30 stocks right away, and it's always 30 stocks and gets rebalanced every Monday, where Alpha Picks, it could have two to three trades a month.

As I said, the 1st and the 15th will have the Buys. Occasionally, there's a Sell. So there's only two to three trades where with the PRO Quant Portfolio, that's about the average on a weekly basis. There's about two to three trades versus per month. The stock universe is also far broader. For PQP, we're looking at U.S. stocks plus ADRs, plus all market cap levels. For Alpha Picks, we do not buy stocks that are below $500 million. The stocks are U.S.-listed securities, with the occasion of a primary ADR that could be listed on a U.S. exchange.

There is no share price minimum for the PRO Quant Portfolio. And for Alpha Picks, we do not buy stocks under $10. Trade alerts get sent out on a weekly basis with detailed analysis for PQP. And for Alpha Picks, it's twice a month. And as I said, the turnover is two to three positions a week, where the turnover for Alpha Picks is significantly lower.

Emma, let me open it up to questions.

EJ: Awesome. Two things, folks. I see a lot of questions about bundled offers for all of our products. It's a great question. I'm going to speak to some folks on the back end and see how best we can communicate those offers with you when we post the replay.

So I believe you should get an email that highlights when this replay is posted and that transcript will ideally include the bundled offers. Thanks so much for inquiring about that. It's a great question.

Steve, you answered, I think a lot of people's questions around the difference between Alpha Picks and PRO. So thank you for that.

This question is kind of an amalgam of questions we got. We get a lot about some of the difference between the PQP and Alpha Picks portfolio and the gap up that can happen with small cap names.

We covered it a bit in the conversation earlier, but is there a trade-off between small cap names and portfolio volatility? Maybe you could speak to PQP's unique value prop with those small cap names relative to Alpha Picks.

SC: So no question about it. When you have small cap, you have to be tolerant of increased volatility, but there is a payoff to small caps. They're undiscovered companies many times, or they could be in an industry that's really starting to take off, or they might have a unique technology in an existing industry that they are mastering and growing faster than other companies. But often when a company is in a growth stage like that, they typically might have more leverage and debt than a larger cap company.

So sometimes it could be interest rate sensitive in the small cap sector overall. If interest rates are going up, small cap stocks tend to underperform. Having said that, when interest rates come down, small cap stocks tend to perform better. So we might be hitting a really nice cycle for small cap stocks if we find that rates are coming down.

But no question about it, there is volatility. However, as I mentioned, over the long-term, diversification can really be your friend. So we look for diversification amongst factors, such as Value and Growth. But we're also looking at diversification for small cap and large cap, as well as U.S. and international. And that maximum diversification, although at times it comes with more volatility, it usually has better returns over a long period of time.

EJ: Well said. This is another process-oriented question. Short question, I've just signed up for PRO. Are we getting invited for regular calls to discuss the portfolio and new picks versus buys?

SC: Well, we do have is, every Monday at 9:30, the analysis goes out. Typically with the calls, we're doing it every other week for the webinars. And it's something that increasingly seems like it's popular. We can move that to weekly. Right now, though, with the number of people that are signing up, it seems to be working on a basis of twice a month.

EJ: Awesome. I've queued up some of the live questions here. Great question about how we think about a top-down versus bottom-up model. In the PQP portfolio, is there some consideration given to diversity of sectors or industries to prevent over-concentration in certain sectors?

SC: Great question. And the answer is yes. We actually have built into the model. It is proprietary. We don't give the trigger amount, but if it is being weighted too heavily towards a specific sector, it will prevent stocks from that sector coming into the portfolio and it will go to the next best stock in the next sector. So we do pay attention to the concentration risk.

EJ: Great. Now, I think you covered this a bit, but maybe your elevator pitch of why an Alpha Pick subscriber might change to PRO.

SC: Greater diversification. You're getting international stocks and you're getting far more smaller cap stocks in PRO than you get in Alpha Picks.

EJ: Great. And another kind of rebalance methodology question. Does PRO let winners run even when they fall to Hold similar to Alpha Picks? AP keeps them for 180 days, I believe.

SC: That is absolutely correct. Alpha Picks will keep a stock in there for 180 days. It really does let the winners run. In the case of PRO, it lets the winners run as long as it's a Strong Buy or Buy. We'll keep a Hold in there for a much lower amount of time than we do with Alpha Picks.

And the reason why is since we're rebalancing on a weekly basis and we're averaging two to three new ideas per week, we tend to really focus on the Strong Buy stocks. So we don't have the Holds in there quite as long.

EJ: Great. Makes sense. And then I think lastly, since we're coming up on time, are there ETFs or funds that simulate PQP?

SC: There are not. Thank you for asking that question. Most likely we will not issue any type of ETF. Seeking Alpha is not an asset manager, and we're not a broker dealer, we’re not an investment bank. We provide independent research and you subscribe to our service to get that investment research.

So, our sort of belief at this point, and I don't see that changing anytime soon, is we will not be entering the asset management field. We like providing investment research. It's fun.

EJ: Yeah, it'd be a bit of a different regulatory status too if we became an asset manager, but…

SC: Absolutely.

EJ: Folks, I loved the participation on the chat. Everyone's so enthusiastic. We are up on time, but I encourage you to redirect some of your questions to the replay that will be posted and we'll as always try to answer there.

SC: I guess, Emma, I will say at the end, if you do not have the PRO Quant Portfolio or PRO, if you are on the Seeking Alpha platform, all you have to do is look for PRO. You'll see that on the left-hand menu, or you'll see it on the very top about how to subscribe. And you could choose either Premium or PRO. Once you subscribe to PRO, you automatically get the PRO Quant Portfolio product.

Thank you so much for attending this webinar today. We wish you all the success in the world as you make your investment decisions. And hopefully, our tools and resources will help make you better investors. Thank you very much.

EJ: Thanks, all. Thanks for your time today and take care.
